front: Film: used NASA-designed Zeiss lenses (f/0.7 f-stop) to shoot scenes entirely by candlelight
Film: throughout the film, the camera slowly zooms out to create painting-like tableaux inspired by William Hogarth
Film: final shot recreates the last piece in Hogarth's Marriage-a-la-Mode; the drunk-slump scene was inspired by The Tête-à-Tête
Film: cinematographer John Alcott won the Academy Award for Best Cinematography for this film
Film: main title and duel scenes feature Handel's D minor Sarabande; battle scenes use 'The British Grenadiers'
Film: son Bryan's eighth birthday party features a magic show (rabbit in a drawer) set to Schubert's German Dance No. 1; Bryan later dies thrown from a horse
Film: climactic duel in a dilapidated barn with cooing pigeons; protagonist loses his left leg
Film: last image is a freeze frame of Barry getting into a carriage; narrator calls him 'utterly baffled and beaten'
Film: grew out of Kubrick's abandoned Napoleon biopic; made between A Clockwork Orange and The Shining
